evals.expected_output: The skill detects all changed files, groups them by type+scope (separating test files from implementation), proposes well-formed conventional commit messages with bodies where the diff reveals non-trivial changes, validates against commitlint or commitizen if configured, presents the full plan, and waits for approval before executing.
expectations: The skill runs git status or git diff to detect changed files before proposing anything
Test files are separated into their own commit(s) from implementation files
Each proposed commit message follows the type(scope): subject format
The skill presents the plan and waits for user approval before executing any git commit

evals.prompt: commit, I replaced the setTimeout retry with async/await to fix the race condition in the login flow
evals.expected_output: The skill uses the user's stated rationale as the primary source for the commit body. The body explicitly captures the decision: replacing setTimeout with async/await to fix a race condition. The subject line reflects the change type (fix or refactor) and scope derived from the modified files.
expectations: The commit body references the user's rationale: async/await replacing setTimeout to fix a race condition
The commit type is fix or refactor (not feat), matching the nature of the change
The scope is derived from the modified files (e.g. auth, login)
The skill does not invent rationale — it uses what the user provided

evals.expected_output: The skill proposes exactly 2 commits: one for login.ts (implementation, typed as feat/fix/refactor based on the diff) and one for the test file (typed as test), keeping them separate per the test/implementation separation rule.
expectations: login.ts is in a commit with type feat, fix, or refactor — not test
login.test.ts is in a separate commit with type test
Both commits use the same scope derived from the file paths (e.g. auth)
